title: Does intelligence ‘emerge’ in large language models?
gist: SFI researchers John Krakauer, Melanie Mitchell, and David Krakauer propose a complexity-science framework arguing that scaling-driven performance gains in LLMs do not constitute genuine 'emergence' absent a qualitative change in internal mechanism, and that current AI has capabilities but not intelligence; a companion study by Alison Gopnik links human world-model formation to a process called 'empowerment gain.'

insights.claim: A new framework co-authored by John Krakauer (Johns Hopkins), Melanie Mitchell, and David Krakauer (both SFI) holds that an AI system only exhibits genuine 'emergence' if it shows a qualitative change in its internal mechanism for solving a task, not merely improved performance as it scales.
insights.speaker: John Krakauer
insights.so_what: Gives a falsifiable test to separate genuine emergent-capability claims from scaling-driven marketing narratives used to justify AI capex.
insights.quote: It needs to show a qualitative change in the internal mechanism with respect to how the task is done.
insights.locator: It needs to show a qualitative change

insights.claim: Merely getting better at a task as an LLM scales does not, by itself, qualify as an 'emergent property' under this SFI-linked framework.
insights.speaker: John Krakauer
insights.so_what: Pushes back on the common industry claim that scale alone produces emergent breakthroughs, a premise underlying many scaling-law-based AI investment theses.
insights.quote: Just getting better at something doesn't make you have an emergent property.

insights.claim: The paper's authors note that widely-cited 'sudden' emergent skills in LLMs are not accompanied by any identified internal reorganization of the model, casting doubt on whether these are true emergent phenomena rather than artifacts of how capability is measured.
insights.speaker: Santa Fe Institute
insights.so_what: Undercuts hype around 'sudden capability jumps' often cited to justify continued scaling spend; suggests some reported jumps may be measurement artifacts, not phase transitions.
insights.locator: proponents of AI claim that certain AI capabilities

insights.claim: Melanie Mitchell argues LLMs display a 'collection of capabilities' but not intelligence, which she defines as the general ability to adapt a concept to new, untrained situations and to acquire new abilities efficiently — something these systems generally cannot do.
insights.speaker: Melanie Mitchell
insights.so_what: Sets a named-researcher benchmark for what would actually count as AGI-grade intelligence, useful for judging future model releases' AGI claims against a rigorous bar.
insights.quote: A collection of capabilities is not intelligence.

insights.claim: The paper draws a hard line between 'emergent capabilities,' which LLMs can arguably develop, and 'emergent intelligence,' which no AI system has achieved to date.
insights.speaker: Melanie Mitchell
insights.so_what: Frames current frontier models as capability-rich but not intelligent by this definition, a data point against near-term AGI timelines used to justify aggressive AI capex.
insights.locator: the authors have emphasized the difference between emergent capabilities

insights.claim: John Krakauer is skeptical that 'world model' is a coherent, useful construct for evaluating AI, arguing the term has become more popular than it is useful.
insights.speaker: John Krakauer
insights.so_what: A credentialed skeptic on a term widely used to hype AI systems' 'understanding' of the world — worth citing when discounting world-model claims in AI product marketing.
insights.quote: It is not at all clear to me what a world model is — a term far more popular than it is useful.
insights.locator: "It is not at all clear to me

insights.claim: David Krakauer proposes that the best way to think about a world model is as an 'emergent explanation' characterized by compression rather than exhaustive simulation — 'less is more.'
insights.speaker: David Krakauer
insights.so_what: Offers a compression-based alternative framework for judging whether an AI system genuinely 'understands' its domain, relevant to interpretability-focused research bets.
insights.quote: Perhaps the best way to think about a world model is as an emergent explanation, in which, “less is more”
insights.locator: Perhaps the best way to think about a world model

insights.claim: A companion study by Alison Gopnik (UC Berkeley) and colleagues found that 'empowerment gain' — engaging in actions that let a person vary and control outcomes — helps both adults and children learn to associate actions with consequences, based on two experiments testing human world-model formation.
insights.speaker: Alison Gopnik
insights.so_what: Identifies a concrete candidate mechanism (empowerment gain) for how humans build world models, a possible design target for researchers trying to instill grounded world models in AI agents.
insights.locator: A different paper in the themed issue co-authored by

insights.claim: The emergence and world-model debate is being formally adjudicated in a themed issue of Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society A titled 'World models in natural and artificial intelligence,' signaling growing academic scrutiny of AI capability claims beyond industry benchmarks.
insights.speaker: Santa Fe Institute
entities: Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society A
insights.so_what: A venue to track for further rigorous academic critiques of AI capability claims, a counterweight to industry-driven AGI narratives.
insights.locator: In the themed issue, “World models in natural

insights.claim: Melanie Mitchell argues that determining whether and what kind of world model an LLM has could explain erratic behaviors like hallucinations and could point the way toward building more trustworthy, explainable AI.
insights.speaker: Melanie Mitchell
insights.so_what: Ties the abstract emergence/world-model debate to a concrete commercial problem — hallucination reliability — that gates enterprise AI adoption.
insights.quote: If an LLM has a human-like model of the world with all of the understanding that goes with it, it might help it become more trustworthy and also explainable to people.
insights.locator: Mitchell says that understanding the kind of world model

top_entities: Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society A
